title: Troubleshooting cards for n8n deployment incidents
acceptanceTarget: For wrong webhook URL, lost credentials, database connection failed, and secure cookie error, the responder can state the first check and repair direction without guessing.

cards.name: wrong webhook URL
cards.symptom: The external service calls localhost, an old domain, an HTTP URL, or reports a bad webhook URL.
likelyCauses: WEBHOOK_URL is unset or points to the wrong public base URL
Reverse proxy exposes HTTPS but n8n generated an internal HTTP URL
Workflow was reactivated before URL configuration was corrected
cards.firstCheck: Compare the Webhook node Production URL in the editor with WEBHOOK_URL and the actual public domain.
cards.repairDirection: Set WEBHOOK_URL to the HTTPS public base URL, set N8N_PROXY_HOPS when behind a proxy, restart n8n, then reactivate or re-register the webhook.
cards.prevention: Keep WEBHOOK_URL in deployment secrets and verify displayed webhook URLs after any domain or proxy change.

cards.name: lost credentials
cards.symptom: After migration, container recreation, or worker rollout, credentials cannot decrypt or OAuth tokens fail unexpectedly.
likelyCauses: N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY changed
Original .n8n settings file was not preserved
Workers use a different encryption key than main
cards.firstCheck: Verify the active N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY against the original production key before changing credentials.
cards.repairDirection: Restore the original N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY or restore from a backup that includes the matching key; if the key is permanently lost, recreate affected credentials.
cards.prevention: Store the instance encryption key in a managed secret store, include it in restore drills, and require the same key on main, workers, and webhook processors.

cards.name: database connection failed
cards.symptom: n8n startup logs show database connection failed, or /healthz is reachable while /healthz/readiness is not ready.
likelyCauses: DB_TYPE or DB_POSTGRESDB_HOST is wrong
PostgreSQL password, database name, port, or SSL settings are wrong
n8n and PostgreSQL are not on the same Docker network
cards.firstCheck: Check DB_TYPE=postgresdb and every DB_POSTGRESDB_* value, then test /healthz/readiness.
cards.repairDirection: Correct PostgreSQL host, port, database, user, password, SSL, and Docker network; restart n8n only after DB is healthy.
cards.prevention: Pin DB env in .env, run readiness checks after deploy, and include DB restore and connection tests in release gates.

cards.name: secure cookie error
cards.symptom: Login session does not persist, browser rejects cookies, or secure cookie behavior fails behind a proxy.
likelyCauses: Public access uses HTTP while N8N_SECURE_COOKIE remains true
TLS terminates at the proxy but X-Forwarded-Proto is missing or wrong
Local testing copied production cookie settings without HTTPS
cards.firstCheck: Confirm the browser URL is HTTPS and the final proxy sends X-Forwarded-Proto=https to n8n.
cards.repairDirection: For production, fix TLS and forwarded headers; for local-only HTTP testing, set N8N_SECURE_COOKIE=false and revert before public exposure.
cards.prevention: Keep production on HTTPS, test proxy headers during deploy, and separate local-only env from public env.

cards.name: OAuth callback mismatch
cards.symptom: The provider rejects OAuth login with redirect URI mismatch or invalid callback.
likelyCauses: Provider redirect URI does not match the n8n OAuth Redirect URL
WEBHOOK_URL or N8N_EDITOR_BASE_URL points to a stale domain
Proxy rewrites scheme or host
cards.firstCheck: Copy the OAuth Redirect URL displayed by n8n and compare it byte-for-byte with the provider redirect URI.
cards.repairDirection: Correct the n8n public URL or provider redirect URI, then reconnect the credential through the n8n UI.
cards.prevention: Document OAuth redirect URI for each credential and retest after domain, proxy, or protocol changes.

cards.name: proxy forwarded headers
cards.symptom: IP whitelist, scheme detection, websocket, or client IP behavior is wrong behind a reverse proxy.
likelyCauses: N8N_PROXY_HOPS is not set
X-Forwarded-For, X-Forwarded-Host, or X-Forwarded-Proto is missing
Multiple proxies changed the expected hop count
cards.firstCheck: Inspect the last proxy configuration and confirm n8n receives the correct forwarded headers.
cards.repairDirection: Set N8N_PROXY_HOPS to the number of trusted proxies and configure the final proxy to pass X-Forwarded-* headers.
cards.prevention: Include proxy header tests in every public URL change and keep a route map for /webhook, /webhook-waiting, and /webhook-test.

cards.name: DNS or TLS failure
cards.symptom: The public domain does not resolve, TLS certificate fails, or HTTPS health checks fail.
likelyCauses: DNS A, AAAA, or CNAME record points to the wrong target
Reverse proxy certificate does not cover the hostname
Firewall or load balancer is not forwarding 443
cards.firstCheck: Resolve the hostname and run an HTTPS header check against /healthz.
cards.repairDirection: Fix DNS records, proxy virtual host, certificate coverage, or firewall forwarding before changing n8n workflows.
cards.prevention: Track DNS TTL, certificate renewal, and proxy route ownership in the deployment runbook.

cards.name: container restart loop
cards.symptom: The n8n container restarts repeatedly or the UI cannot be reached after deploy.
likelyCauses: Invalid env value
Volume permission or mount issue
Port collision
Image tag or package startup failure
cards.firstCheck: Check docker ps, restart count, and the first startup error in docker logs.
cards.repairDirection: Fix the startup error, env file, mount, permission, or port mapping; do not delete data volumes during first response.
cards.prevention: Run deploys with health checks, keep env validation, and document volume names.

cards.name: resource exhaustion
cards.symptom: Users see 503, Connection Lost, or workflows stop with memory-related messages.
likelyCauses: Workflow fetches or transforms too much data in one execution
Binary data is handled in memory
Concurrency is higher than available CPU or memory
cards.firstCheck: Inspect memory, CPU, OOM events, and the execution that was running when the error occurred.
cards.repairDirection: Reduce concurrency, split heavy workflows, adjust binary data storage, prune executions, or increase resources.
cards.prevention: Add resource alerts, define workflow payload limits, and review heavy binary workflows before production.

cards.name: queue worker stalled
cards.symptom: Executions wait in queue, workers do not process jobs, or queue mode incidents only affect production executions.
likelyCauses: Redis connection failed
Workers cannot reach PostgreSQL
Workers do not share the main N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY
Worker concurrency and DB pool are mismatched
cards.firstCheck: Check worker logs, Redis env, DB env, and shared N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY across main and workers.
cards.repairDirection: Fix Redis, DB, shared encryption key, worker command, or concurrency; restart workers after main and DB are ready.
cards.prevention: Keep main and workers on the same version, expose worker health checks, and monitor queue metrics.

cards.name: webhook path or method conflict
cards.symptom: n8n reports that a webhook path is already in use, or Production URL does not trigger while Test URL works.
likelyCauses: Another active workflow owns the same path and HTTP method
The workflow is not active
The caller uses Test URL behavior for a production workflow
cards.firstCheck: Confirm workflow active state, webhook path, HTTP method, and whether the caller uses Test URL or Production URL.
cards.repairDirection: Deactivate the conflicting workflow, change path or method, activate production workflow, and update the external caller.
cards.prevention: Maintain a webhook registry with path, method, owner, active workflow, and external caller.

cards.name: missing logs or evidence
cards.symptom: The team cannot determine which process, node, or request caused the incident.
likelyCauses: N8N_LOG_LEVEL is too low for the incident
Logs are only local container stdout and were rotated away
Main, workers, proxy, DB, and Redis logs are not correlated
cards.firstCheck: Confirm log output, format, retention, and whether the incident timestamp exists in logs.
cards.repairDirection: Enable structured logs, centralize process logs, add DB logging during investigation when needed, and write an incident note with exact timestamps.
cards.prevention: Use JSON logs, centralized retention, incident note discipline, and monitoring links in every alert.
